coredata.dc:title: Visible light-responsive mechanically and electronically controllable conductive carbon dot-hydrogel-based pressure-strain sensor for wireless monitoring of antifouling performance
coredata.dc:creator: Shit, Arnab
Giang, Nguyen Ngan
Park, Sung Young
coredata.dc:description: This work describes the visible-light-induced wireless pressure and strain sensor based on carbon dot (CD) loaded conductive hydrogel for the mechanical force-regulated antifouling behavior. The conductivity, as well as pressure and strain sensitivities of the hydrogel, were modulated by modifying the hydrophobic–hydrophilic transformation of the CD components that controlled the antifouling effect through mild mechanical forces, allowing the cells to be squeezed out from the hydrogel. The photocatalytic activity of the CD containing carbonized polydopamine (cPDA) releases the cPDA which enhances the network formation, resulting in a four-fold higher conductivity, 158% enhanced mechanical strength, and 231% improved stretchability, which enhances the pressure and strain sensing response. Furthermore, antifouling phenomenon involved in the physical deformation associated with the changes in the electrochemical behavior of the hydrogel, which was monitored by wireless sensing, demonstrated the effect of mechanical force on the antifouling behavior for the successful removal of cells from the hydrogel. Additionally, the efficacy of the antifouling effect depending on pressure or strain was evaluated by real-time monitoring of the wireless response. This antifouling property of the hydrogel together with visible-light-controlled hydrophobic–hydrophilic transformation can allow the development of hydrogel-based electronics for electronic skin sensors, prosthetics, and biosensing applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Smart materials
Multifunctional composites
Nanoparticles
Sensing
Electro-mechanical behavior

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of cPDA/TiO2@f-cPDA/TiO2 (CD) loaded PVA/TA hydrogel (CD-hydrogel)

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Basic Science Research Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea funded by the Ministry of Education (Grant No. 2020R1A2B5B02001500 and 2018R1A6A1A03023788 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic illustration of the CD-hydrogel with visible light treatment showing hydrophobic-to-hydrophilic transformation with enhanced stretchability and wireless pressure sensing capability. (b) Hydrodynamic sizes of the nanoparticles and CD were measured with DLS depending on the visible light treatment time. (c) PL spectra of the CD with visible light treatment. (d) XRD spectra of the CD before and after 9 h visible treatment. (e) TEM image, SAED pattern, and HR-TEM image of the CD before (left side) and after visible light treatment (right side). (f) AFM image of the CD-hydrogel coated surface before (left side) and after (right side) visible light treatment.

(a) Nyquist plots of the CD-hydrogel obtained with visible light treatment. (b) Tensile property of the CD-hydrogel depending on the visible light treatment. (c) Visual stretchability with visible light irradiation. (d) Strain sweep measurements of the CD-hydrogel at a constant frequency of 1 Hz as a function of visible light irradiation time. Dynamic rheological test data was obtained by cyclically varying the strain (e) before visible light irradiation and (f) after visible light irradiation. (g) DSC thermogram of the CD-hydrogel.

Performances of strain sensors assembled using CD-hydrogels. (a) Change in capacitance as a function of strain and linear fitting curve. (b) Response and recovery time of the CD-hydrogel sensor. (c) Cyclic response of pressure with different loadings. (d) Cyclic response of strain with different loadings. (e) Cyclic stability of finger bending motion before and after visible light treatment. (f) Stepwise finger bending movement sensing of the CD-hydrogel before and after visible light treatment. (g) Wireless pressure and strain sensing using finger movement.

(a) Physical methods for the detachment of cells after visible treatment. (b) Nyquist plot, and (c) source meter resistance to measure the antifouling performance. (d) Wireless response of the antifouling performance, the bottom ribbon represents the relative antifouling value of the CD-hydrogel (compared with CD-hydrogel after all cells removed). (e) The strain sensing response of the antifouling performance. (f) Live and dead cell assay using CLSM imaging of HeLa cells after detached from the surface using visible light (scale bar 100 μm). [The antifouling performance are summarized as follows: (1) CD-hydrogel without visible light treatment, (2) cell embedded CD-hydrogel, cell embedded CD-hydrogel with (3) no motion, (4) pressing, (5) bending, (6) stretching, (7) twisting respectively, with visible irradiation.].

head.ce:title: Visible light-responsive mechanically and electronically controllable conductive carbon dot-hydrogel-based pressure-strain sensor for wireless monitoring of antifouling performance

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical and Biological Engineering, Korea National University of Transportation, Chungju, 27469, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical and Biological Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical and Biological Engineering, Korea National University of Transportation, Chungju, 27469, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of IT and Energy Convergence (BK21 FOUR), Korea National University of Transportation, Chungju, 27469, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of IT and Energy Convergence (BK21 FOUR)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of IT and Energy Convergence (BK21 FOUR), Korea National University of Transportation, Chungju, 27469, Republic of Korea

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Department of Chemical and Biological Engineering, Korea National University of Transportation, Chungju, 27469, Republic of Korea.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical and Biological Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This work describes the visible-light-induced wireless pressure and strain sensor based on carbon dot (CD) loaded conductive hydrogel for the mechanical force-regulated antifouling behavior. The conductivity, as well as pressure and strain sensitivities of the hydrogel, were modulated by modifying the hydrophobic–hydrophilic transformation of the CD components that controlled the antifouling effect through mild mechanical forces, allowing the cells to be squeezed out from the hydrogel. The photocatalytic activity of the CD containing carbonized polydopamine (cPDA) releases the cPDA which enhances the network formation, resulting in a four-fold higher conductivity, 158% enhanced mechanical strength, and 231% improved stretchability, which enhances the pressure and strain sensing response. Furthermore, antifouling phenomenon involved in the physical deformation associated with the changes in the electrochemical behavior of the hydrogel, which was monitored by wireless sensing, demonstrated the effect of mechanical force on the antifouling behavior for the successful removal of cells from the hydrogel. Additionally, the efficacy of the antifouling effect depending on pressure or strain was evaluated by real-time monitoring of the wireless response. This antifouling property of the hydrogel together with visible-light-controlled hydrophobic–hydrophilic transformation can allow the development of hydrogel-based electronics for electronic skin sensors, prosthetics, and biosensing applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Owing to its high water content and similar characteristics of human tissue, hydrogels have been applied in various fields such as electronic skin, strain or force sensors, smart actuators, and wearable electronic devices [1–3]. Regrettably, due to the scarcity of dynamic inflection and complex structure, most traditional hydrogels are affected by the deficit of stimuli‐responsive functional groups, poor mechanical strength, and high electrical resistance, which restrict their applications in several inventive fields such as wearable pressure–strain sensors [4,5]. Therefore, the formation of electrically conductive hydrogels with balanced mechanical and stimuli-responsive properties is extremely challenging to realize a mechanically tunable biosensor. Nanocomposite hydrogel is a well-accepted model for hydrogel systems owing to its tunable mechanical, electrical, and stimuli-responsive properties and facile fabrication processes [6,7]. From a materials design standpoint, all types of nanomaterials including nanodots, nanoparticles, nanotubes, and nanosheets can potentially be integrated into the hydrogels [8]. From a functional design viewpoint, in addition to conductivity, multi-functionality is essential for real-world applications in wearable devices and antifouling detectable electronics [9]. Nanocomposite hydrogels are also suitable in terms of their antifouling activity because functional nanoparticles control the antifouling characteristics of the hydrogel. However, current methods used for antifouling involve harsh conditions and a time-consuming process (24 h–7 d) to remove the foulant that deteriorates the properties of the hydrogel [10]. Therefore, exploration of stimuli-responsive nanoparticles and designing well-defined hydrogel network structures are important for the rational design of multifunctional nanocomposite hydrogels where the antifouling property can be achieved using simple techniques.

The sp2-carbon-rich carbon dots (CD) are a relatively new class of compounds with excellent surface modification and tunable properties as well as biocompatibility, which have been established as potential candidates for electrically conductive materials for hydrogels [11–13]. The modification of the CD with stimuli‐responsive functional groups alters the dynamic surface properties, which can be stimulated using heat, light, reagent, or pH [13–15]. Among these, light-responsive CD are the most attractive candidates owing to their capability to induce dimensional changes without changing the chemical environment [16]. Thus, the incorporation of light-responsive, switchable hydrophobic–hydrophilic CD with structural changes into the hydrogel matrix is a useful strategy to obtain a visible-light-responsive multifunctional hydrogel with tunable mechanical and electrical properties [17].

Herein, a method to fabricate a visible-light-responsive CD (cPDA/TiO2@f-cPDA/TiO2)-loaded conductive hydrogel with the mechanical force-controlled antifouling property is described, where TiO2-immobilized carbonized polydopamine‐perfluorosilica (f-cPDA/TiO2) and carbonized polydopamine (cPDA/TiO2) are utilized. The CD-loaded physically cross-linked polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) was penetrated into tannic acid (TA) to obtain a CD-incorporated PVA/TA hydrogel (CD-hydrogel) which showed enhanced conductivity, mechanical stretchability, physical strength, and rheological characteristics after visible light treatment. The visible-light-induced and mechanical-force-triggered antifouling behavior was monitored using wireless communication, which confirmed the applicability of the hydrogel as a novel material for designing biosensors such as electronic skin.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of cPDA/TiO2@f-cPDA/TiO2 (CD) loaded PVA/TA hydrogel (CD-hydrogel)
ce:section.ce:para: To prepare the CD, first 10 mg f-cPDA/TiO2 and 50 mg cPDA/TiO2 first dispersed in a mixed solution of 8 mL distilled water and 2 mL ethanol for 15 min followed by stirring for 30 min. After preparing the CD, 335 mg TA was added and stirred at 70 °C for 2 h. Then 1 g PVA (Mw∼ 150000) was added and stirred at 90 °C for 3 h until the full dissolution of PVA. The viscous solution was then mold cast on hot condition. After cooling to room temperature 3 freeze cycles were performed at −50 °C for 18 h and room temperature 6 h.

To investigate the antifouling properties of the synthesized CD-hydrogel, HeLa cell suspension of 105 cells/mL were cultivated on CD-hydrogels for 24 h at 37 °C in a humidified environment with 5% CO2. Thereafter, the cell solution was removed and the cell-cultured CD-hydrogels were washed with PBS (pH 7.4) to get rid of the non-adhered cells. The cell-cultured CD-hydrogels were subsequently irradiated under visible light to determine the antifouling capability. Stretching, bending, twisting, and stretching were performed after irradiation of visible light to evaluate the role of mechanical force on the cell removal process.

To achieve visible-light-controlled enhancement of mechanical and electrical properties, a TiO2-deposited cPDA and f-cPDA-loaded conductive CD-hydrogel is constructed to control the wireless monitoring of antifouling performance by mechanical force. The visible-light-induced changes in the particle and tuning properties of the CD-hydrogel are shown in Fig. 1 a and Scheme S1. The large-sized cPDA/TiO2-decorated f-cPDA/TiO2 nanoparticles are loaded into the PVA/TA network to obtain a visible-light-responsive conductive CD-hydrogel. With the exposure of visible light, the association of the particles becomes weak which releases the cPDA inducing the hydrophobic–hydrophilic transformation and formation of hydrogen bonds within the CD-hydrogel network, thereby increasing the mechanical strength and dimensional stability. Simultaneously, the small particles generated show enhanced solubility, the hydrogen bond-promoted delocalization of charge carriers thereby increasing the electronic conductivity of the CD-hydrogel. In the absence of light, the particle size remains large with relatively low solubility, which restricts the release of cPDA, thereby decreasing the conductivity and mechanical strength. Thus, a visible-light-controlled CD-hydrogel was prepared to realize a wireless pressure and strain sensor.

Accounting for the visible-light-induced changes in the conductivity and mechanical strength of the CD-hydrogel, the pressure–strain sensor was further constructed to evaluate the light-induced changes in the system. As shown in Fig. 3 a, tension deformation of the sensor generally increases the relative capacitance variation (ΔC/C0). Based on the linear fitting curve, a gauge factor (GF) of 1.69 is obtained, which is comparable to some hydrogel-based sensors previously (
Table S1
). The strongly crosslinked conductive network of the pressure sensors also gives it an unusually fast reaction time (152.5 ms) and recovery time (183.7 ms) for a 20 kPa stress stimulus (
Fig. 3
b), which is faster than several previously reported hydrogel-based pressure sensors [30,31]. The ΔC/C0 ratio increases dramatically with increasing pressure, as shown in Fig. 3c, and the pattern of reaction under each cycle pressure is the same, proving that the sensor can discriminate between pressures using steady sensing signals. Fig. 3d depicts the sensor's cyclic strain detecting behavior at various strain levels. The sensor responds consistently and quickly to diverse strain levels, exhibiting its superior capacity to discriminate across strains. If the same deformation is applied to the sensor and kept constant, a step of the same capacitance value is obtained, which means that the sensor exhibits excellent reversibility and is restored to a negligible hysteresis [32]. The fabricated strain sensor mounted on the index finger joint is stretched and relaxed repeatedly for application as a human motion sensor. The sensor that has not been exposed to visible light breaks after 2240 sensing cycles, while the sensor subjected to visible light exposure responds efficiently to periodical tension strain, up to 3000 cycles (
Fig. 3
e) demonstrating the dependability of these strain sensors [32]. Fig. 3f displays the relative capacitive response of the sensor mounted on the index finger joint while the joint is bent gradually from 0 to 90°. The bending of the finger is suitably monitored by variation in the capacitance and the value of the relative capacitance change distinguishes various bending states [33]. Thus, such flexible strain sensors may detect any sort of bending motion that causes deformation of the composite CD-hydrogel. Additionally, the generated electrical signals from the pressure and strain sensing responses of the CD-hydrogel were monitored by Smartphone through wireless transmission (
Fig. 3
g and
S14
). Similarly, the CD-hydrogel without visible light treatment shows a low response to pressure and strain while after 9 h of visible light treatment, the CD-hydrogel shows comparatively higher pressure and strain responses. Furthermore, the hydrogel sensors were sensitive to minute physiological signals, such as voice recognition by wireless transmission. The hydrogel sensor applied to the neck can discriminate between distinct texts (Fig. S15a). Surprisingly, unimodal signals were recorded when the word “hi” was said, bimodal signals were obtained when the word “hello” was spoken, and trimodal signals were recorded when the phrase “banana” was spoken. The facial expression was also successfully distinguished while smiling and fawning (
Fig. S15b
). However, the peaks of the electrical signal were basically persistent after the visible light treatment, whereas the non-treated sample only show an undistinguishable low-intensity signal pattern due to lack of sensitivity. This suggested that the CD-hydrogels might be useful in active rehabilitation and voice recovery.

Furthermore, the hydrogel was utilized to evaluate the antifouling property under controlled mechanical force. The antifouling behavior under visible light irradiation has been conventionally conducted by continuously pipetting the cells that have been subjected to harsh and vigorous conditions, which has harmful effects on cell viability and cell migration [34,35]. Additionally, long-term pipetting to remove the cells from the hydrogel can destroy the network of the CD-hydrogel as a high force is exerted on the CD-hydrogel [36]. Therefore, mild mechanical-force-based stimuli applied to the CD-hydrogel surface were employed to achieve favorable and convenient cell detachment [37]. In brief, HeLa cells at a concentration of 105 cells/mL were cultured on the CD-hydrogel surface under the reported conditions and subsequently exposed to visible light irradiation. Thereafter, different mechanical forces (pressing, bending, stretching, and twisting) were applied onto the CD-hydrogel surface in a PBS bath (Fig. 4 a). The effectiveness of each deformation method for cell detachment was monitored and assessed by the change in the resistance values of the CD-hydrogels, as determined by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). The resistance values of the deformed CD-hydrogels after visible light irradiation were measured in advance by several electrochemical methods (Figs. S16a–c), which did not show any noticeable change in the resistance depending on the mechanical force. Similarly, the EIS measurements of the cell-cultured CD-hydrogels subjected to visible light treatment with complete cell removal by the deformation actions of >100 cycles were examined. In both cases, the resistance values (Fig. S17a) remain unaltered during physical deformation under the same conditions, suggesting the occurrence of the complete antifouling effect of the CD-hydrogel after 100 cycles of deformation conducted in a PBS bath. The change in resistance of the cell-detached hydrogel after antifouling was confirmed using a wireless system that corresponded to the resistance values of the EIS measurements, indicating the complete detachment of cells under visible light irradiation (Fig. S17b). Furthermore, to evaluate the cell removal capability of each deformation, 30 cycles of each deformation were performed in PBS (Fig. 4a). As shown in Fig. 4b, pressing and bending the cell-cultured CD-hydrogels does not afford effective cell detachment as their resistance values are significantly higher than that of the visible-light-treated CD-hydrogel, which is ascribed to the rigidity of the CD-hydrogel after visible light irradiation. On the other hand, the twisting and stretching performances display a favorable method for cell removal with the decrease in resistance values compare to the values of pressing and bending performance which proved the detachment of the cells out of the CD-hydrogel. This phenomenon can be explained by investigating the morphology of the hydrogel by scanning electron microscopy. Due to the decrease in the pore size accompanying the enlarged surface area, the mechanical force of twisting increases the pressure of the absorbed PBS buffer, which “squeezes out” the cells effectively (Fig. S18). A similar trend for the change in resistance is also observed for the measured resistance by a source meter, as shown in Fig. 4c. To quantify and evaluate the antifouling behavior of the physical-deformation-triggered cell detachment, the resistance response obtained via wireless measurements was examined (
Fig. 4
d). Among the physical deformation methods, twisting affords the highest relative antifouling of 100%, whereas stretching, bending and pressing result in 95, 84, and 65% antifouling, respectively, indicating that twisting is an effective deformation method for antifouling. In comparison with the light-treated CD-hydrogel, only 42% antifouling was achieved for the motion-free treatment. The strain sensing performance of the CD-hydrogel was further evaluated after physical-deformation-triggered antifouling using the wireless and LCR method to evaluate the finger bending phenomenon, which showed an intense electrochemical signal with an increase in the antifouling activity (Fig. 4e). In addition, wireless strain sensing was performed to confirm the electrochemical response of the cell-detached hydrogel following the mechanical motions that exhibited a similar trend to the LCR method, demonstrating the efficiency of twisting for cell removal (Fig. S19). However, no changes in the electrochemical responses are observed in CD-hydrogels without cell culture and completely cell-removed CD-hydrogels (Fig. S16 d, e, and S17c) which can be correlated to the change in resistance that occurs during the antifouling process as explained earlier. To determine the number of detached cells, the cell counting was done using fluorescence-activated Cell sorting (FACS) after antifouling by twisting (Fig. S20a, b). The cell viability of HeLa cells after the antifouling effect was investigated by the live and dead cell assay performed using confocal imaging, demonstrating green fluorescence toward a small amount of red signals, which proved the biocompatibility of the CD-hydrogel and cell survival under visible light irradiation with the assistance of mechanical force-based stimuli ( Fig. 4
f,
S21 ). The live and dead cell assay was subsequently performed using FACS to confirm the cell viability after cell detachment by visible light irradiation which showed >99% alive cells after mechanical force treatment ( Fig. S20
c, d). The comparison of the antifouling performance of CD-hydrogel is summarized in Table S2 , which quantifies the feasibility of the designed system. These results provide compelling evidence for the fabrication of an antifouling CD-hydrogel with visible-light-induced hydrophobic–hydrophilic transformation for a mechanically tunable pressure sensor. To achieve light responsiveness, many hydrogels were coupled with chromophore components, but The photoresponsive system demands that light stimuli have wavelengths in the UV or near-infrared region, which can be hazardous to living things and result in a slow response, poor mechanical properties, and limited functionalities. The conductive CD-hydrogel has visible light tunable pore size which can show the visible light-triggered mechanical force regulated antifouling performance. Therefore, our visible-light responsive CD-hydrogel is a preferable option of environmentally sensitive hydrogels in designing antifouling and human-machine interfaced devices displays for the visually impaired.

We have developed visible-light-induced tunable solubility of CD into hydrogel with controlled conductivity, mechanical strength, and pressure-strain sensitivity for mechanical force-regulated antifouling behavior. The visible light-stimulated photocatalytic effect transforms the large particle of CD to a smaller size with the release of cPDA, thus, the hydrogen bond-promoted delocalization of charge carriers resulted in a 4-fold improvement of conductivity (5.2 mS m−1) with 121% enhanced capacitance response. The improvised hydrophobic-hydrophilic transformation progressed the swelling and H-bonding interaction within the CD-hydrogel, enhancing the stretchability (231%) and mechanical strength (0.83 MPa) by extending the viscoelastic region to 78% strain. Consequently, the tunable mechanical and electrical behavior enables reversible pressure/strain sensing with a GF of 1.69 with increasing the stability from 2240 to over 3000 strain cycles. Furthermore, the visible light-tuned pore size of the CD-hydrogel was utilized to control the mechanical force-triggered antifouling. The efficacy was evaluated using a wireless system, where the twisting force into CD-hydrogel showed 100% antifouling effect by squeeze out the cells from the CD-hydrogel, whereas the motion-free antifouling process displayed only 42% of inefficient cell detachment. This mechanical force triggered visible light-controlled antifouling behavior of CD-hydrogel can offer an innovative approach for the development of hydrogel-based electronics.

Arnab Shit: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Visualization, Writing - original draft. Nguyen Ngan Giang: Formal analysis, Methodology. Sung Young Park: Conceptualization, Resources, Supervision, Writing - Review & Editing, Funding acquisition, Validation, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Basic Science Research Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea funded by the Ministry of Education (Grant No. 2020R1A2B5B02001500 and 2018R1A6A1A03023788).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.compscitech.2021.109212.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A bioinspired mineral hydrogel as a self-healable, mechanically adaptable ionic skin for highly sensitive pressure sensing

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Lei, Q. Wang, S. Sun, W. Zhu, P. Wu, A Bioinspired Mineral Hydrogel as a Self-Healable, Mechanically Adaptable Ionic Skin for Highly Sensitive Pressure Sensing, Adv. Mater. 29 (2017) 1700321. https://doi.org/10.1002/adma.201700321.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electronic skin: recent progress and future prospects for skin-attachable devices for health monitoring, robotics, and prosthetics

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J.C. Yang, J. Mun, S.Y. Kwon, S. Park, Z. Bao, S. Park, Electronic Skin: Recent Progress and Future Prospects for Skin-Attachable Devices for Health Monitoring, Robotics, and Prosthetics, Adv. Mater. 31 (2019) 1904765. https://doi.org/10.1002/adma.201904765.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Highly stretchable porous composite hydrogels with stable conductivity for strain sensing

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Tuning conductivity and roughness of diselenide polymer dot-coated surface for ROS-mediated selective real-time wireless detection of cancer cells

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S.G. Kim, B. Ryplida, N.N. Giang, G. Lee, K.D. Lee, S.Y.
